title: The Freedom to Be Racist? How the United States and Europe Struggle to Preserve Freedom and Combat Racism By Erik Bleich Oxford University Press. 2011. 224 pages. $84.69 cloth, $24.95 paper
abstract: It would seem all modern, western societies are sharing a harsh legal and social condemnation of racism. But in practice, the question is what happens to this attitude when it conflicts with other important values of our society? How important is antiracism in practice and what are we willing to sacrifice in order to achieve what kind of nonracist society? Erik Bleich, in his book, The Freedom to Be Racist? How the United States and Europe Struggle to Preserve Freedom and Combat Racism, analyses how different countries have dealt with balancing nondiscrimination with fundamental freedoms. He follows three analytical strands: freedom of expression, freedom of association and opinion-as-motive. In each of these topics he explores the historical development of the United States and at least two European countries. Bleich shows the social, historical, political and juridical context in which the fight against racism collides with other fundamental values or simply with technical, political or juridical problems. In carefully selected case studies, the author also examines religious discrimination and discusses, for example, the debate of the Mohamed cartoons that were published in the Danish newspaper Jylland-Posten in 2005, or anti-Semitism, specifically with regards to Holocaust denial.
